planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; ironweed is tolerant of clay soil and seasonal flooding and excels in moist, sunny meadow and rain garden settings.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained to seasonally wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; New York ironweed is a stately native perennial of moist eastern meadows and stream banks that produces large, flat-topped clusters of the most intensely saturated purple-magenta flowers of any native fall wildflower — a color so vivid and jewel-like that it challenges belief — from late summer through fall. The plant grows boldly tall (5–6 feet) and pairs magnificently with tall goldenrods and joe-pye weeds in a classic native late-season meadow composition. Monarchs, swallowtails, bumblebees, and scores of native bees mob the flowers at their peak. Shear stems back by half in late spring to reduce final height and improve branching without sacrificing bloom. The russet seedheads persist through winter, providing food for goldfinches.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; sweet Joe Pye weed tolerates dry conditions better than spotted Joe Pye weed and adapts to lightly shaded woodland edge situations.

directions: Plant divisions or container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; sweet Joe Pye weed is an imposing, architecturally bold native perennial reaching 6–9 feet tall in rich, moist soil, producing large, domed, vanilla-scented, mauve-pink to rose-purple flower clusters from late summer through fall that attract remarkable numbers of monarch butterflies, tiger swallowtails, and native bees. The whorled, vanilla-scented leaves and purple-flushed stems are ornamental throughout the season. Cut back stems by one-third in late spring (Chelsea chop) to produce more compact plants without sacrificing the flower display. Excellent at the back of large borders, along pond edges, and in rain gardens. 'Gateway' (shorter, more compact, richer color) and 'Little Joe' (only 4 feet tall) are excellent for smaller gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; anise hyssop self-sows reliably and can be used as a short-lived perennial that renews itself annually from seed in suitable conditions.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; anise hyssop is a native prairie perennial with strongly anise-scented leaves and stems that produces upright spikes of densely packed lavender-blue flowers from midsummer through fall — arguably the single most attractive plant for native bumblebees in the temperate garden, hosting extraordinary numbers of pollinators at peak bloom. The leaves make an excellent herbal tea with a pleasant, sweet anise flavor and can be used fresh in salads and fruit dishes. Plants are short-lived perennials or biennials that self-sow reliably to maintain a colony. Deadhead half the spent spikes to extend bloom; leave the rest for self-seeding and winter finch food. Very drought-tolerant. 'Blue Fortune' (robust, long-blooming hybrid) is the most widely grown cultivar.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant corms or container plants in moist, well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in spring; dense blazing star tolerates clay and periodic wet feet better than most prairie plants and performs reliably in heavy southern soils.

directions: Plant corms or container plants in moist, well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; dense blazing star is the most popular and adaptable liatris species, producing grass-like clumps of foliage topped with striking, bottlebrush-like spikes of fluffy, bright magenta-purple flowers from mid-summer into fall — uniquely, the flowers open from the top of the spike downward (most spikes open bottom-to-top), creating a dynamic visual effect as the bloom progresses. Monarch butterflies use it as a critical nectaring and migration staging plant. Goldfinches eat the seeds through winter. The corms multiply over time to form large clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years in spring. Tolerates clay and seasonal flooding better than most prairie plants. 'Kobold' (compact, 24 inches, rich color) and 'Alba' (white) are fine cultivar selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; New Zealand iris prefers cool, moist coastal conditions and may struggle in hot, dry summer climates without supplemental irrigation.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; New Zealand iris forms upright, fan-shaped clumps of narrow, rigid, dark-green, iris-like foliage that is evergreen in mild climates and produces clusters of pure white, three-petaled flowers in late spring followed by bright orange seed capsules that persist through fall — providing three distinct seasons of ornamental interest. The neat, architectural foliage clumps are excellent for edging paths, front-of-border placement, and contemporary garden designs. Excellent drought resistance once established but benefits from supplemental water during extended hot, dry spells. In zone 8 the foliage may be damaged by hard freezes; cut back in early spring and new growth re-emerges rapidly.
